one_line_takeaway: A medical-imaging benchmark showing that approximate machine-unlearning (random relabeling, saliency/SalUn) matches exact retraining on retain/forget sets but hurts generalization -- worse at larger forget sizes -- and can be class-biased, while measuring only AUROC and never an actual privacy attack.
key_finding: Approximate unlearning matches exact unlearning on the retain and forget sets but consistently degrades the test set (generalization), and the gap widens with forget-set size. Concrete example: on TissueMNIST-ResNet-18 at 30% forgetting, exact test AUROC is 91.50 vs 89.44 (random relabeling) and 89.78 (saliency); retain also drops (97.51 exact vs 94.82 / 95.04). The methods can be class-biased: at 15% forgetting on TissueMNIST, random relabeling underperforms badly on the EASY class forget split (forget AUROC 92.19 vs 95.82 exact) while saliency unlearning collapses on the HARD class test split (79.44 vs 83.90 exact). In contrast, no gender bias was observed on CheXpert (male vs female AUROC within ~0.2 points for every algorithm/split). Approximate methods also add a hyperparameter-tuning cost that exact unlearning avoids.
core_contribution: An empirical, proof-of-concept benchmark of approximate machine-unlearning algorithms in the medical-imaging domain, which the authors argue had not been studied before due to the specifics of medical data (class imbalance, multiple samples per patient). Using exact unlearning (retrain-from-scratch on the retain set) as the gold-standard baseline, the paper evaluates two approximate algorithms -- random relabeling and saliency unlearning -- across two datasets (TissueMNIST, CheXpert) and two backbones (ResNet-18, ResNet-50) along three axes: (1) effect of forget-set size, (2) per-class effect (easy/intermediate/hard classes), and (3) gender fairness. The stated conclusion is that approximate unlearning is promising but not yet practical for medicine because it degrades generalization and can be class-biased. Note: random relabeling and saliency unlearning (SalUn) are prior methods the authors adopt, not new algorithms; the contribution is the evaluation and the medical-domain framing, not a new unlearning technique.
threat_model: The paper is privacy-motivated rather than adversary-motivated. It frames unlearning around the GDPR Article 17 'right to be forgotten': a patient may request that a medical-imaging model forget the influence of their samples, and the data holder (hospital) must produce an unlearned model plus delete the raw records. The paper cites membership-inference and privacy-leakage attacks as the reason unlearning matters, but it does NOT itself run any attack or adversarial privacy evaluation. Effectiveness is measured purely by AUROC on retain/forget/test splits (an accuracy proxy), and the authors explicitly list membership-inference-attack (MIA) success rate as future work. So the security relevance is real (privacy / regulatory compliance) but the paper does not verify unlearning against a concrete adversary.
limitations: Effectiveness is judged only by AUROC on the splits -- no membership-inference or any privacy/adversarial attack is run, so the paper does not actually demonstrate that private influence was removed (a false-sense-of-privacy risk the authors' own cited ref [30] warns about). Only two prior approximate algorithms are evaluated (random relabeling, saliency/SalUn); only ResNets; only one multi-class-single-label dataset (TissueMNIST, and only 50,000 of its 165,466 train images) and one binary-multi-label dataset (CheXpert). No vision transformers, no multi-class-multi-label dataset. Fairness is limited to binary gender on one dataset. It is a proof-of-concept benchmark, not a new method; the reported gains/losses are AUROC deltas, not statistical-significance tests beyond reported std.

datasets_used: TissueMNIST (histopathology, 8 classes; 165,466 train / 23,640 val / 47,280 test, but only 50,000 train images used; 128x128, pixels /255)

CheXpert (chest radiographs; 223,414 train, 5,285 val, 5,025 test split by patient ID; resized 320x390 -> 224x224; U-one policy, 5 binary labels: Atelectasis, Cardiomegaly, Consolidation, Edema, Pleural Effusion)
quantitative_results: TissueMNIST-ResNet-18, 30% forget, Test AUROC: Exact 91.50 vs Random Relabeling 89.44 vs Saliency 89.78 (approximate degrades generalization)
TissueMNIST-ResNet-18, 30% forget, Retain AUROC: Exact 97.51 vs Random Relabeling 94.82 vs Saliency 95.04 (only case where retain drops notably)
TissueMNIST-ResNet-18, 5% forget, Forget AUROC: Exact 91.80, Random Relabeling 91.29, Saliency 91.50 (competitive on forget set)
CheXpert-ResNet-50, 30% forget, Test AUROC: Exact 77.10 vs Random Relabeling 76.19 vs Saliency 76.12
Per-class TissueMNIST 15% forget, EASY class Forget AUROC: Exact 95.82 vs Random Relabeling 92.19 (random relabeling class-biased)
Per-class TissueMNIST 15% forget, HARD class Test AUROC: Exact 83.90 vs Saliency 79.44 (saliency class-biased)
Fairness CheXpert 15% forget, Test AUROC male/female: Exact 77.24/77.43, Random Relabeling 76.57/76.61, Saliency 76.85/76.90 (no gender bias)
baselines_compared: Exact unlearning (reinitialize + retrain from scratch on retain set) -- the gold-standard baseline
Random relabeling (relabel forget set, fine-tune 2 epochs) -- approximate method under test
Saliency unlearning / SalUn (gradient-magnitude weight masking + relabel, fine-tune 2 epochs) -- approximate method under test
